text:
Admission Date:  2009-09-26              Discharge Date:   1999-11-25

Date of Birth:  2010-10-06             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Scott
Chief Complaint:
Transfer from OSH for sepsis

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1. 1985-10-20: CT guided aspiration of T5-6 paraspinal fluid
collection.
2. 1985-10-20: Thoracentesis.

History of Present Illness:
Mrs Yu is a 49 yo woman with a medical history notable only
for hypertension who was in her usual state of good health until
10 d prior to admission to an OSH when she started having
fevers, nausea, and back pain. She treated herself with Motrin
and continued going to work until 3 d PTA. On 1971-10-14, she was
brought home early by coworkers b/c she was not feeling well and
by 6:00 pm that night she was becoming increasingly confused and
complained of visual symptoms ("unable to see anything, lights
flashing") and her family called EMS. At the OSH she was unable
to follow commands, became combative, and was given Ativan.
Initial labs at the OSH included WBC 17.3, hct 41, plt 112, BUN
22, creat 1.2, Albumin 1.8.
She had an LP, which showed WBC 208 (82 polys, 8 lymphs, 10
monos), RBC 3, Glu 49, prot 83 and an opening pressure of 18
cmH2O. A CSF gram stain showed no polys, no bacteria and a
negative culture. A urine culture grew MSSA and 05-20 blood
cultures also grew MSSA.  An echo at the OSH was of poor quality
due to the patient's agitation, and showed focally thichened
aortic valve, but no vegetations, valvular abnormalities, or
pericardial effusions. She was initially treated with
vancomycin, ceftriaxone, and acyclovir, and was transferred to
Reagan Memorial Hospital for further management.


Past Medical History:
1. HTN.
2. Probably had chickenpox as a child according to mother.

Social History:
She lives with her husband and two children (aged 16 and 20) in
USNV Smith
FPO AE 63147, MA.  She formerly worked as a 5th and 6th grade math
teacher but for the past year has worked as a secretary for a
general contractor.  Has had three sexual partners with no known
history of sexually transmitted diseases.  She has no history of
blood transfusions.  She states that she drinks 2-3 glasses of
wine per night.  No history of smoking or IV drug use.

Family History:
1. CAD.
2. HTN.
3. Daughter has Sz disorder.
4. No history of immunodeficiency.

Physical Exam:
Vs: Tm 100.3, BP 114/65 on 0.04 Levophed, HR 82, RR set at 12,
AC FiO2 50%, TV  set at 500.
Intubated, sedated
HEENT: R small conjuctival hemorrhage.
RRR, no MRG
CTAB
S/NT/ND
Extr: No 1631 Pope Harbor
Montgomeryfurt, DC 01783. Numerous lesions over palms and soles reminiscent
of endocarditis stigmata (splinter hemorrhages, Lester
lesions, Osler nodules).
Skin: In addition to above, has several vesicular lesions
diffusely over all extremities, some look herpetic/crusted.

Pertinent Results:
Labs at discharge:
wbc: 9.7
hematocrit: 33.0
platelet: 342
Na 140 K 3.7 Cl 103 CO2 24 BUN 8 Cr 1.1 glucose 127 Ca 9.4 Mg
1.7 Ph 5.0

ALT 6 AST 13 LDH 286 Alk Phos 157 T bili 0.4
ESR 66

Microbiology:
04-16 - 08-19 Blood cultures: no growth.
03-04 urine clx: no growth.
03-29 urine clx: > 100,000 yeast.
08-22 urine clx: > 100,000 yeast.
05-01 urine clx: 10-100,000 enterococcus (vancomycin resistant).
08-19 urine clx: mixed bacterial flora consistent with
contamination.

07-19, 10-06, 01-06, 05-01 stool: negative for C. difficile.

10-23 pleural fluid: no PMNs, no organisms on gram stain, negative
culture.

10-23 paraspinal abscess: 1+ PMNs, no organisms on gram stain,
rare methicillin sensitive staph aureus.

EKGS:
2004-01-28:
Normal sinus rhythym.
Rate PR  QRS QT/QTc
90   132 78  394/441.71

2013-02-15:
Normal sinus rhythym.
Rate PR  QRS QT/QTc
87   134 82  412/456.42

Studies:
10-28 Trans-esophageal ECHO:
The left atrium is dilated. No spontaneous echo contrast or
thrombus is seen in the body of the left atrium/left atrial
appendage or the body of the right atrium/right atrial
appendage. No atrial septal defect is seen by 2D or color
Doppler. Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and
systolic function are normal (LVEF>55%). [Intrinsic left
ventricular systolic function may be more depressed given the
severity of valvular regurgitation.] The aortic valve leaflets
(3) appear structurally normal with good leaflet excursion. No
masses or vegetations are seen on the aortic valve. No aortic
regurgitation is seen.  The mitral valve leaflets are mildly
thickened. There is a large (1cm x 3cm long) highly mobile)
vegetation is seen within the left atrium attached to the base
of the anterior mitral valve leaflet. The posterior mitral
leaflet appears somewhat rudimentary, but without discrete
vegetation. No mitral valve
abscess is seen. An eccentric jet of moderate to severe (3+)
mitral
regurgitation is seen (posteriorly directed) originating at the
base of the anterior leaflet (suggests perforation). There is no
pericardial effusion.
Compared with the prior TEE study (tape reviewed) of 1976-01-11,
the anterior mitral leaflet vegetation appears slightly larger
and the posterior leaflet is less "rudimentary." The degree of
mitral regurgitation has significantly increased.

06-13 Trans-thoracic ECHO:
The left atrium is mildly dilated. There is mild symmetric left
ventricular hypertrophy. The left ventricular cavity size is
normal. Overall left ventricular systolic function is normal
(LVEF>55%). Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion
are normal. The aortic valve leaflets (3) appear structurally
normal with good leaflet excursion and no aortic regurgitation.
The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. There is a large
vegetations on the mitral valve (one on the anterior leaflet and
one on the posterior leaflet). Moderate (2+) mitral
regurgitation is seen. The mitral regurgitation jet is
eccentric. There is a very small pericardial effusion.
Compared with the prior study (tape reviewed) of 1988-05-31,
mitral
regurgitation is now more prominent and vegetations are now more
prominent.

04-02 CT of ABD, PELVIS:
1.  Persistent terminal ileitis.  Etiology includes infectious
versus
inflammatory process.
2.  Persistent large bilateral pleural effusions with bibasilar
compressive atelectasis.
3.  Small free fluid in the pelvis, but no evidence of abscess.

02-13 Trans-thoracic ECHO:
1. The left atrium is mildly dilated.
2. Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and systolic
function are normal (LVEF>55%). Regional left ventricular wall
motion is normal.
3. The mitral valve appears structurally normal with trivial
mitral
regurgitation. There is a large (2 x 1 cm) , mobile vegetation
on the mitral valve.
4. Compared with the findings of the prior study of 1976-01-11,
there has been no significant change.

06-07 CT of CHEST, ABD, PELVIS:
1.  Interval increase in size of bilateral large pleural
effusions with
worsening bibasilar compressive atelectasis. Diffuse ground
glass opacities within the lungs are likely due to pulmonary
edema.
2.  Ill-defined, new, nodular opacities within both upper lobes,
which are
non- cavitating.  These are likely inflammatory or infectious in
etiology.
3.  Interval development of ascites and marked third-spacing
fluid within the subcutaneous tissues.
4.  Stable appearance of hypodense lesion within the left
kidney, which may represent a cyst or area of scarring.
5.  Tiny amount of gas within the subcutaneous tissues of the
left abdominal wall at the level of the umbilicus.  There is no
abscess noted.
6.  Intramural fat and wall thickening involving the terminal
ileum, which
appears worse in the interval. No bowel obstruction or
surrounding fat
stranding. These findings suggest a chronic inflammatory process
involving the terminal ileum such as Crohn's disease; clinical
correlation is suggested.
7.  Bilateral enhancing paraspinal fluid collections at the T5/6
level, likely secondary to discitis. No associated osseous
destruction noted.

10-08 ECHO:
1. Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and systolic
function are normal (LVEF>55%).
2. There are simple atheroma in the aortic arch and descending
thoracic aorta.
3. There is a large vegetation (2.5 x 0.7 cm) attached on the
left atrial side to the lateral portion of the mitral annulus
and the basal portion of the posterior leaflet of the mitral
valve. The vegetation is mobile and prolapses into the left
ventricle during diastole. No mitral valve abscess is seen.
Trivial mitral regurgitation is seen.

10-08 MRI of L-spine:
No evidence of bone, disc or intraspinal abnormality to suggest
infection of the lumbosacral spine. There is no lumbosacral
neural impingement.

10-08 MRI of HEAD:
Multiple foci in the right cerebellum, right splenium, and right
superior cerebral cortex of signal abnormality and diffusion
signal hyperintensity are consistent with areas of infarction
and perhaps infection. Findings may be compatible with an early
infection, and septic emboli should be considered, particularly
in view of the patient's staph aureus bacteremia.

10-08 MRI of C-spine, T-spine:
Findings are consistent with osteomyelitis and discitis with
epidural and paraspinal infection at the T5-6 level. There are
slight abnormalities in the C5-6 disc space and C5 vertebral
body, though there are no clear signs of infection in this
location at this time.

03-04 U/S of ABD:
1. Normal gallbladder and bile duct.
2. Coarse liver echo texture without focal lesions.

03-04 CT of HEAD:
Hypodense lesions, as described above, most pronounced in the
right cerebellar hemisphere and right splenium of the corpus
callosum. The differential diagnosis includes evolving
infectious process, possibly representing hematogenous spread,
infarction, and neoplasm.

03-04 CT of CHEST, ABDOMEN:
1. Bilateral dependent areas of atelectasis/consolidation with
associated small pleural effusions. The possibility of
aspiration is raised given the reflux of contrast noted in the
esophagus.
2. The questioned right mid thoracic paraspinal abnormality is
not well assessed given the adjacent acute abnormalities and
fluid. Differential considerations include loculated pleural
fluid versus benign cystic structures, although underlying
pathology is difficult to exclude. No adjacent bony destruction
is seen.
3. No evidence of abscess within the abdomen or pelvis.

Procedures:
1985-10-20 CT-GUIDED ASPIRATION OF RIGHT T5/T6 PARASPINAL COLLECTION
1. 2 cc of purulent material aspirated from the right T5/T6
paraspinal soft tissue density in keeping with some abscess
component with
that area.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mrs. Yu is a 49 yo woman who was transferred from OSH with
Methicillin sensitive staph aureus bacteremia complicated by MV
endocarditis, multiple septic emboli to brain, T5/6 diskitis,
and disseminated zoster infection.
.
1Walkericillin sensitive staph aureus bacteremia:
This was of unclear etiology as she denied any IV drug use,
recent instrumentation, or recent cutaneous injuries.  She was
noted to have a crusted over lesion on her right foream which
looked suspicious for a source but she did not recall the
duration of this lesion.  On admission to the ICU she initially
required blood pressure support with IV fluids and pressor
agents.  These were eventually weaned off and she was able to
maintain her pressure.  Imaging revealed evidence of
dissemination with endocarditis (MV vegetation), T5-6
osteomyelitis with a paraspinal fluid collection, and cerebral
emboli.  She was started on oxacillin 2 grams IV every four
hours and should complete a six week course (should finish on
03-10).  She had slow clinical improvement and denied
specific symptoms but had persistent fevers while on oxacillin.
This was pursued with repeat imaging of the chest, abdomen, and
pelvis on 06-07 which revealed large bilateral pleural effusions
and the continued presence of a fluid collection around T5-T6.
A thoracentesis was performed on 10-23 which revealed a
transudative fluid.  A CT guided aspiration of the paraspinal
fluid collection was also performed on 10-23 which revealed 1.5 cc
of purulent fluid that contained rare methicillin sensitive
staph aureus.  A TTE was done on 02-13 which showed an unchanged
mitral valve vegetation.  Her fevers declined somewhat following
the drainage of her paraspinal fluid collection but she did not
completely defervesce.  A repeat abdominal CT was performed on
04-02 to look for an occult abscess and to follow up terminal
ileum thickening seen on the 06-07 study.  This CT revealed
persistent bilateral effusions and terminal ileum thickening but
no abscesses.  On 04-02 she was noted to have a new 2-3/6 LLSB
ECHO and a TTE done on 06-13 showed an increase in the size of
her MV vegetation, more prominent mitral regurgitation, and a
small pericardial effusion.  Both cardiology and CT surgery were
consulted and she underwent a TEE with anesthesia on 10-28 to
better characterize her valve and rule out an abscess. This
revealed that her vegetation was more prominent and her valvular
regurgitation was more prominent as well (please see results
sections for more details).  CT surgery would like to replace
her valve surgically but would like for her to complete as long
of an antibiotic course as possible before surgery is
undertaken.  Thus she is being discharged to home on IV
antibiotics with the following f/u appointments:
1. She will see Dr. [**Last Name (Prefixes) **] in CT surgery on 07-20 to discuss
the timing of her valve replacement.
2. She will see one her cardiologist, Dr. May, before her
surgery to arrange for a cardiac catheterization prior to her
surgery.
3. She will see Dr. Rodriguez in the ID department on 03-10 to
discuss the duration of her antibiotic treatment.  At this
appointment, the ID department may also consider repeat imaging
of her spine to assess if her T5-T6 osteomyelitis has resolved.
4. She will need to have dental clearance prior to her cardiac
surgery.  She knows this and will arrange for this near her
home.
5. While on oxacillin she should have weekly blood work
including a CBC, chem 7, LFTs.  She should also have regular
EKGs to test for conduction abnormalities.  EKGs done in the
hospital showed no acute changes.  Her lab results should be
faxed to Stephanie Andrews, MD in the infectious disease department at
Reagan Memorial Hospital 238-151-1439.
6. When available, she should have a flu shot.

2. Hypertension:
She came into the hospital with the diagnosis of hypertension
but her toprol was initially held.  As she came out of the MICU
her blood pressure began to rise and she was eventually
re-started on Toprol Xl which was titrated up to 100 mg daily.
At the suggestion of cardiology, she was also started on
captopril for afterload reduction given her mitral
regurgitation.  This was titrated up to 25 mg TID and switched
to Lisinopril 10 daily for ease of pill taking.  By the end of
her hospitalization her systolic blood pressure was in the low
140s.  She was discharged on Toprol XL 100 daily and Lisinopril
10 mg daily.   Her blood pressure should be followed up by her
primary care physician as she will likely need further titration
of her meds to attain a goal SBP in the 120s.

3. Left sided weakness:
She was noted to have left arm weakness (more pronounced in her
deltoid and biceps) throughout her hospitalization.  This was
thought to be due to her right sided cerebral emboli.  Her
strength gradually improved throughout her hospitalization with
regular physical therapy.  At discharge she was still generally
weak below her baseline and was encouraged to maintain physical
therapy as an outpatient.

4. Diarrhea:
She did complain of loose, non-bloody stools intermittently
throughout her hospitalization.  C. difficile toxin tests were
negative multiple times.  Abdominal CT scans showed terminal
ileum thickening thought to be consistent with either an
infectious or inflammatory process.  She denied any personal or
family history of inflammatory bowel disease.  By the end of her
hospitalization her diarrhea had resolved somewhat.  Further
workup of her diarrhea and TI thickening should be considered by
her PCP.

5. Anemia:
Her hematocrit on admission was 33.2.  Throughout her
hospitalization her hematocrit was in the mid to high twenties
and she required three transfusions for Hct less than 25.  Iron
studies were consistent with anemia of chronic disease. By the
end of her hospitalization, her hematocrit was 33.

6. Pleural effusions:
She was noted to have pleural effusions on CT scan and was
started on lasix 20 mg po daily on 08-19.  She responded with a
brisk diureses and improvement in her oxygenation.  She was
continued on Lasix 20 po daily at discharge.  This should be
followed up by her PCP as she will likely not need lasix
chronically.

7. Vision changes:
She had reportedly complained of vision changes at the outside
hospital.  Once she was extubated she no longer complained of
vision trouble and had an unremarkable vision exam.
Ophthalmology was consulted and found no evidence of
endopthalmitis.  They did note bilateral superficial hemorrhages
perhaps secondary to hypertension and recommended artificial
tears for comfort.  She was encouraged to use artificial tears
on a prn basis as an outpatient.

8. Zoster:
Her skin exam on admission was notable for multiple herpetiform
clusters of erythematous erosions across her right buttock as
well as on her extremities and back.  Dermatology was consulted
and recommended a seven day course of IV acyclovir.  A DAT for
VZV and HSV 1 and 2 was negative but her lesions crusted over
following completion of acyclovir.

9. Low albumin:
Her albumin on admission was 1.9.  The etiology of this was
uncertain as her liver functions were otherwise normal and her
urine only had trace protein.  It was thought that her low
albumin may have been due to poor nutrition.  She should have a
repeat albumin level drawn by her PCP once her bacteremia has
resolved.

11.  Yeast/Enterococcus in urine:
She was noted to have >100,000 yeast in a urine culture drawn on
03-29.  This persisted after her foley catheter was removed.  She
was also noted to have skin breakdown and a candidal type rash
in the peri-genital area that only improved minimally with
topical antifungal creams and powders.  Thus she was treated
with a three day course of fluconazole (finished 10-24) and her
skin improved.  A repeat urine culture on 05-01 grew 10-100,000
enterococcus which were resistant to vancomycin.  However, she
was without symptoms and thus was not treated.  A repeat urine
culture drawn on 08-19 was consistent with contamination.  At
discharge she had no urinary symptoms.

Disposition:
She was discharged to home in stable condition with resources to
help her continue taking IV methicillin every four hours as an
outpatient.  She was also encouraged to continue physical
therapy as an outpatient to continue to increase her strength.
She has follow up appointments with infectious disease,
cardiology, and CT surgery.  She will see her PCP in the near
future for a blood pressure check as well as bloodwork including
a CBC, chem 7, and LFTs.

Medications on Admission:
Toprol XL

Discharge Medications:
1. Calcium Carbonate 500 mg Tablet, Chewable Sig: One (1)
Tablet, Chewable PO BID (2 times a day).
2. PICC care
10 ml NS followed by 2 ml of 100 Units/ml heparin (200 units
heparin) each lumen Daily and PRN.
3. Oxacillin Sodium 2 g Recon Soln Sig: One (1) Recon Soln
Injection Q4H (every 4 hours) for 15 days.
Disp:*60 Recon Soln(s)* Refills:*0*
4. Metoprolol Succinate 100 mg Tablet Sustained Release 24HR
Sig: One (1) Tablet Sustained Release 24HR PO once a day.
Disp:*30 Tablet Sustained Release 24HR(s)* Refills:*2*
5. Lisinopril 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
6. Furosemide 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
7. Polyvinyl Alcohol-Povidone 1.4-0.6 % Dropperette Sig: 12-12
Drops Ophthalmic  QID (4 times a day).
Disp:*qs ml* Refills:*2*


Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
Gentiva

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary diagnosis:
1. MSSA bacteremia
-MV vegetation.
-T5-6 osteomyelitis and paraspinal abscess.
-Septic right sided cerebral emboli.
Secondary Diagnosis:
1. Hypertension.


Discharge Condition:
Stable.


Discharge Instructions:
1. You are being discharged home with services to help with your
antibiotic therapy.
2. Please continue to take your medications as prescribed - you
will need to take IV oxacillin every four hours for a total of 6
weeks - this will finish on 07-09.
3. Please continue physical therapy at home to maintain and
increase your strength.
4. If you experience increasing fevers, increasing difficulty
breathing, chest pain, new weakness or numbness, or other
concerning symptoms, please seek medical attention.

Followup Instructions:
1. You have an appointment with your PCP, Jones. Stephanie Gray
(763-902-4485) on Simpson, 09-06 at 10:00 AM.
2. Provider: Rebecca Walker Joseph Wood, MD Where: LM Kindred Rehabilitation Hospital Clear Lake DISEASE Phone:478-489-2292 Date/Time:2017-04-20 9:30
3. Provider: Rebecca Walker,[**Last Name (Prefixes) 413**] CARDIAC SURGERY LMOB 2A Where:
CARDIAC SURGERY LMOB 2A Date/Time:1974-12-15 1:30
4. You will have an appointment with your cardiologist Dr.
May at a later date. They will call you to arrange an
appointment.  IF you have not heard from them by the end of the
week please call 143-883-4538 to schedule an appointment.  They
will arrange for you to have a cardiac catheterization prior to
your cardiac surgery.


                             Stephanie Gray MD 87995

Completed by:1999-11-25
